This is an operation that doesn't get as much love, but I think is a really useful tool in any hernia surgeon's toolbox.

iPOM versus iPUM, they're actually describing the same operation.
Historically, it's called intraperitoneal onlay mesh, but this poorly describes the actual operation.
Intraperitoneal underlay mesh technique is a more accurate description.
A lot of people will call it iPOM plus or iPOM plus, distinguishing traditional bridge underlay from the more contemporary approaches with defect closure.
I think that defect closure is a critical part of the success of the operation.
Proposed drawbacks of the iPOM technique include possibility for increased pain with fixation, increased cost with coated meshes,
the potential for mesh visceral interactions because of the intraperitoneal nature of the mesh, and some say maybe higher rates of recurrence.
The truth is that the larger data sets that have looked at this and even some prospective studies have not demonstrated all of these to necessarily be true.

A lot of surgeons now use alternatives such as a transabdominal preperitoneal approach or retromuscular approach, though these are a little more invasive, or open preperitoneal or open onlay approaches for smaller defects, though for much smaller defects, preperitoneal dissection could be hard without actually making the hernia bigger.
So my justification for maintaining this in my practice is, one, I think it's a great option for smaller defects that easily close.
I think it's a really great option for teaching hernia surgery.
It's a great introduction to preparatory dissection and robotic hernia techniques.
